query: order of caries susceptibility of primary teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Pathophysiology -- Primary Dentition's Enamel
retrieved_docs.content: The enamel of primary teeth is less organized and significantly thinner than in the permanent dentition. Consequently, the enamel of deciduous teeth is demineralized more rapidly than their permanent counterpart. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Nursing caries characteristically affect the four primary upper incisors, whereas lower incisors remain sound. The rest of the teeth may be involved but less severely. These children usually experience pain and eating difficulties.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood caries (ECC) is possibly the most important and common dental disease in pediatric patients. [13] [14] ECC can be described as the breakdown of tooth enamel in a pediatric patient. Often caused by bacterial oral flora, the acid produced in the enzymatic processes involved in carbohydrate breakdown results in the erosion of the soft tooth enamel of primary teeth. [15] Fluoridation in water has drastically reduced the amount of tooth decay in the last 100 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of dental caries in the primary dentition of young children is known as early childhood caries (ECC), [1] [2] which is defined as one or more decayed, missing (resulting from caries) or filled teeth in primary dentition in children of up to 71 months of age. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: All types of caries, including nursing caries, result from the interaction of cariogenic microbiota, fermentable carbohydrates, and susceptible teeth that interact during a prolonged period. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term early childhood caries includes the sometimes called nursing caries and rampant caries. “Baby bottle syndrome,” “nursing caries,” or “bottle mouth caries” are names to describe a pattern of caries where the deciduous upper incisors and molars are more severely disrupted. A key feature of this type of caries is the sparing of the lower incisors, which can be completely healthy or mildly affected. The term rampant caries is usually given to extensive caries affecting children of three to four years of age that do not follow the nursing caries pattern. Despite early childhood caries can be easily preventable, it is one of the most prevalent childhood diseases worldwide, mainly affecting socially disadvantaged populations. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- International Caries Detection and Assessment System
retrieved_docs.content: The ICDAS was developed to include the current knowledge about the pathogenesis of caries development, detecting carious lesions at an early stage, and quantifying the degree of demineralization. The ICDAS features an individualized scoring system to detect and assess primary coronal and root caries and caries associated with restorations and sealants (CARS). Using the ICDAS for root caries is yet to be implemented in epidemiological studies. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology -- Cariogenic Microorganisms
retrieved_docs.content: Children that acquire Streptococcus mutans earlier in life,  even before the first tooth's eruption, are at greater risk of developing caries. This period is called the window of infectivity . If preventive measures are put in place during this crucial period, the risk of developing caries is much lower because less pathogenic bacteria will colonize the oral niches. [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- Decayed-Missing-Filled Index
retrieved_docs.content: The Decayed-Missing-Filled (DMF) index has been the primary indicator of caries experience in the population for more than half a century. [10] It is the sum of the number of teeth (DMFT) or surfaces (DMFS) decayed, missing, or filled in an individual. The 28 permanent teeth are quantified; third molars are typically excluded.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology -- Socioeconomic and Ethnic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood caries is most common in children from a low socioeconomic background: prenatal and perinatal malnutrition predisposes to enamel hypoplasia; [17] [18] [9] [19] [20] a lower level of education and literacy correlates with poor oral hygiene and sugary food consumption; lack of access to dental care; and lack of fluoride exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The first clinical sign of the disease is a white spot, which is the expression of the enamel subsurface demineralization - the surface enamel is more mineralized. If the acid challenge continues, the previously smooth enamel surface becomes rough. Eventually, enamel microcavitations and cavitations may occur - the average time that the carious lesions remain radiographically in the enamel was suggested to be three to four years [14] , but lesions are highly variable, and they have the potential to regress. As the caries process progresses, the dentin also experiences mineral loss and bacterial invasion, producing tertiary dentin to protect the pulp.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Primary teeth are relatively at a larger risk of caries than permanent teeth. They also have larger pulp with prominent pulp horns, which places exact demands on cavity design. Hence, a full-coverage restoration is required. Although stainless steel crowns have an aesthetic disadvantage, they are still the treatment of choice for the following reasons: Durability Efficiency Longevity Cost-effectiveness Reliability Even though other tooth-colored restorations are available in the market, they are costly or require more tooth reduction, increasing the chances of pulp exposure. [15] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe caries with signs and symptoms of irreversible pulpitis or dental sepsis Crown was severely destroyed due to caries and is non-restorable Children at risk of bacterial endocarditis

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of dental caries. Summarize the pathophysiology of dental caries, highlighting the currently accepted theory. Describe dental caries' clinical findings. Explain the treatment considerations for patients with dental caries. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Etiology -- Developmental Enamel Defects
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental enamel defects, such as enamel hypoplasia, have been recognized as a significant risk factor for developing ECC [16] - Streptococcus mutans rapidly colonize the rough defects of the enamel surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth is divided into the crown and the root. [3] [4] The anatomic crown is covered by enamel, while the anatomic root is covered by cementum. Enamel is an avascular, hard material that protects the outer tooth, gives the tooth its whitish color, and is first affected by dental caries. Enamel color is determined by enamel thickness. Enamel is primarily composed of hydroxyapatite and contains no nerves or blood vessels. Owing to their avascularity, they remineralize but do not grow after an acid attack. Enamel can form fluorapatite crystals when exposed to fluoride to become harder and more resistant to caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.
